articles.title: A drug already in trials may finally stop hepatitis E
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists identified bemnifosbuvir as a potential treatment for hepatitis E, which currently has no approved therapy. The drug is already in trials for hepatitis C and was found to block hepatitis E virus replication by disrupting its genetic machinery. Tests in cells and animals showed strong effectiveness without harming healthy tissue. The report notes that if ongoing trials succeed, the drug could be repurposed for hepatitis E.

articles.title: Scientists discover why flu and COVID hit older adults so hard
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reports that aging lungs may contribute to severe outcomes from flu and COVID in older adults. Researchers found certain lung cells can trigger an exaggerated immune response that forms inflammatory cell clusters. These clusters damage lung tissue instead of providing protection. Activating the aging-related signal in young mice made their lungs behave like older ones and led to severe illness.

articles.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed individual immune cells to investigate long COVID. They identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells that was especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID. The report frames this as a clue to persistent fatigue and other symptoms. It positions the finding as a step toward understanding long COVID biology.

articles.title: This diet could slash cholera infections by up to 100x
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reports that diet can strongly influence susceptibility to cholera infection in the gut. Diets rich in certain proteins, especially dairy casein and wheat gluten, reduced cholera bacteria's ability to establish infection. The reductions reached as high as 100-fold in some cases. The proteins are described as disabling a bacterial "weapon" used to attack other microbes and dominate the gut environment.

articles.title: MIT scientists discover gut protein that traps and kills dangerous bacteria
articles.summary_4_sentences: MIT scientists report that a protein called intelectin helps defend the gut. The protein strengthens the intestinal mucus layer and also traps and disables harmful bacteria. It binds sugars on mucus molecules and bacterial surfaces to form a protective barrier. The study notes it can destroy microbes, including some that resist antibiotics.

articles.title: Scientists find gut bacteria inject proteins that control your immune system
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report that gut bacteria can inject proteins directly into human cells using microscopic injection systems. The study suggests these injected proteins can influence immune responses and metabolic pathways. The interactions are proposed to play a role in inflammatory diseases such as Crohn's. The findings are presented as a shift in how scientists understand microbiome influence on human health.

articles.title: Scientists discover “molecular shredder” that helps deadly parasite evade the immune system
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists studying the parasite that causes sleeping sickness identified a protein called ESB2. ESB2 is described as a "molecular shredder" that cuts specific genetic instructions as they are produced. This helps the parasite flood its surface with protective proteins while suppressing other signals that could reveal it. The work explains a mechanism of immune evasion in the bloodstream.

articles.title: Scientists discover tiny rocket engines inside malaria parasites
articles.summary_4_sentences: New research examined spinning crystals found inside malaria parasites. The study reports the motion is powered by a reaction that breaks down hydrogen peroxide and releases energy. This process may help the parasite detoxify harmful chemicals and manage iron. The findings are presented as potentially informing new drugs and inspiring microscopic robotics.

articles.title: Scientists create “smart” DNA drug that targets cancer cells with extreme precision
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists developed a programmable drug system built from synthetic DNA to target cancer cells. The system activates only when it detects a specific combination of tumor markers. This conditional activation is intended to limit damage to healthy tissue. The platform is also described as capable of delivering multiple drugs at once to help address resistance.

articles.title: What’s hiding inside colon cancer could change treatment
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed DNA from over 9,000 patients to assess microbial patterns across cancers. They found that only colorectal tumors consistently hosted distinct microbial communities. The result challenges the idea that all cancers have unique microbial signatures. The study suggests colorectal cancer may have a distinctive microbial fingerprint relevant to diagnosis or treatment.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut signals that could detect cancer early
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reports that gut bacteria and metabolites may support earlier detection of serious digestive diseases. Using AI, scientists found biomarkers linked to one condition could often predict others. The findings suggest digestive diseases may be more interconnected than previously thought. The work is framed as a path toward faster diagnosis without invasive procedures.

articles.title: Scientists find hidden brain cells helping deadly cancer grow
articles.summary_4_sentences: Canadian scientists studied glioblastoma and identified brain cells that can support tumor growth through signaling. These cells were previously thought to only support healthy nerves. Blocking the communication between these brain cells and cancer cells slowed tumor growth in lab models. The results point to a potential vulnerability in glioblastoma biology.

articles.title: Scientists reveal new blood pressure treatment that works when others fail
articles.summary_4_sentences: A global trial tested a pill called baxdrostat in people with uncontrolled high blood pressure. Patients experienced blood pressure reductions of nearly 10 mmHg. The report says the drug targets a hormone involved in salt and water retention. It is presented as a potential option for patients who do not respond to standard treatments.

articles.title: Hardly anyone uses this surprisingly simple blood pressure fix
articles.summary_4_sentences: A national analysis spanning nearly two decades examined U.S. use of salt substitutes. It found that very few Americans use them, including people who could benefit most. The report emphasizes salt substitutes as a low-cost way to reduce sodium intake and improve blood pressure. Researchers describe the low adoption as a missed opportunity to prevent heart disease and stroke.

articles.title: This overlooked hormone could be why your blood pressure won’t drop
articles.summary_4_sentences: A large U.S. study examined people with hard-to-treat high blood pressure and identified elevated cortisol in 27% of them. The report describes this as higher than expected and a possible reason standard medications fail. It suggests the condition may be overlooked in routine care. The findings point toward new testing and treatment approaches for resistant hypertension.

articles.title: Digital heart twins can guide a lifesaving procedure
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers created patient-specific digital heart models to support cardiac ablation procedures. Simulations were used to predict optimal catheter pathways and reduce tissue damage. Clinical trials reported improved procedural success rates. The work is presented as a step toward personalized interventions for complex heart conditions.

articles.title: Scientists say 7 days of meditation can rewire your brain
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reports that one week of intensive meditation and mind-body practices produced measurable changes across the brain and body. Researchers observed improved brain efficiency and boosted immune signaling. Blood measures showed increased chemicals associated with natural pain relief, and the report also notes neuron growth and stronger connectivity. The brain-state changes are described as resembling psychedelic-like patterns without drug use.

articles.title: When our minds wander to the body, it may affect mental health
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study examined how attention directed toward bodily sensations relates to mental health. It reports that body-focused mindfulness was associated with reduced symptoms of depression and ADHD. Brain imaging showed increased connectivity between interoceptive and emotional regions. The authors propose guided body awareness as a potential therapeutic tool for mood-related disorders.

articles.title: These overlooked brain cells may control fear and PTSD
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report that astrocytes play active roles in fear memory rather than serving only support functions. The study found astrocytes interact with neurons in real time during formation, recall, and weakening of fear responses. Manipulating astrocyte activity changed the strength of fear memories. The findings are presented as a possible route toward new anxiety-related treatments.

articles.title: Scientists found a protein that drives brain aging — and how to stop it
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study in aging mice links higher levels of a protein called FTL1 to weaker connections between brain cells and memory decline. Researchers reduced FTL1 and observed recovery in neural connections. The intervention also restored memory performance in the mice. The report frames FTL1 as a potential target for addressing aspects of brain aging.

articles.title: Scientists map the brain’s hidden wiring using RNA barcodes in major breakthrough
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers developed a method that uses RNA barcodes to map neuronal connections. The technique captures thousands of links with single-synapse precision and turns brain mapping into a sequencing task. In mice, it revealed previously unknown connections between brain cells. The study suggests the approach could support earlier detection and targeted treatment of neurological diseases.

articles.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists at Oregon State University observed real-time chemical interactions implicated in Alzheimer's disease. The work focused on how metal ions, particularly copper, trigger harmful protein clumping. The report says these observations provide a clearer molecular picture of disease development. The study emphasizes direct visualization of processes that had been difficult to capture previously.

articles.title: A gene mutation may trap the brain in the wrong reality in schizophrenia patients
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a gene mutation that disrupts a brain circuit involved in flexible decision-making in mice. The mutation caused mice to persist with outdated choices when conditions changed, which the report links to schizophrenia-like difficulty updating beliefs. The affected pathway is described as the thalamus to prefrontal cortex circuit. Reactivating this circuit restored normal behavior in the experiments, suggesting a potential therapeutic direction.

articles.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors. They found the damaged side of the brain appeared to age faster, while the unaffected side could appear younger. The report interprets this asymmetry as the brain attempting to rewire and compensate. The findings describe a measurable change consistent with adaptive remodeling after stroke.

articles.title: This 5-day diet helped Crohn’s patients feel better fast
articles.summary_4_sentences: A clinical trial evaluated a fasting-mimicking diet for Crohn's disease. Participants followed very low-calorie, plant-based meals for five days per month. The trial reported noticeable symptom improvements for most participants. It also found reductions in biological markers of inflammation linked to the disease.

articles.title: Binge drinking just once a month may triple your risk of liver scarring
articles.summary_4_sentences: A large U.S. study examined heavy drinking patterns in people with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). It found that heavy drinking even once a month was linked to a much higher risk of serious liver scarring. MASLD is described as affecting about one in three adults. The report challenges the assumption that occasional binge drinking is harmless in otherwise moderate drinkers.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden brain switch that tells you to stop eating
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a pathway involved in the brain's fullness signaling after a meal. The study reports that glucose activates tanycytes, which signal to astrocytes that then activate fullness neurons. Astrocytes are described as playing an active role rather than only supporting neurons. The findings are presented as potentially relevant to new obesity and eating-disorder treatments.

articles.title: Scientists say BMI gets it wrong for over one third of adults
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study compared BMI categories with body fat measurements from DXA scans. It found that more than one-third of adults were classified incorrectly by BMI. Some people labeled overweight or obese did not have corresponding body fat levels, while others were missed. The report argues this misclassification can affect health assessment and risk identification.

articles.title: This simple Japanese eating habit could help you live longer without dieting
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article describes hara hachi bu, a practice of eating until about 80% full. It emphasizes slowing down and paying attention to hunger cues rather than strict dieting. The report says research suggests the habit may reduce calorie intake and support healthier food choices. It also suggests it may help prevent long-term weight gain.

articles.title: New microwave frying technique could make french fries much healthier
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists tested a cooking method combining standard frying with microwave heating. The approach reduced oil absorption during cooking while aiming to preserve taste and crispness. The mechanism is described as microwaves affecting pressure inside the food to push oil out. The report says the method can cook faster and lower fat content in fries.

topic_groups.topic: Genetics, Reproductive Health and Sensory Disorders

articles.title: Scientists discover reversible male birth control that stops sperm production
articles.summary_4_sentences: A mouse study tested a compound called JQ1 as a nonhormonal male contraceptive approach. The compound temporarily shut down meiosis, stopping sperm production. After treatment ended, sperm production resumed and fertility returned. The animals produced healthy offspring, supporting reversibility in the study.

articles.title: Deafness reversed: One injection restores hearing in just weeks
articles.summary_4_sentences: A small study tested a gene therapy for people born deaf. Researchers delivered a working copy of a hearing-related gene into the inner ear using a single injection. All ten participants, including children and adults, showed improved hearing. Some improvements were reported within one month.

topic_groups.topic: Public Health Policy, Environment and Health Risks

articles.title: Supreme Court ruling on ‘conversion therapy’ puts medical talk in the hot seat
articles.summary_4_sentences: The Supreme Court issued a decision restricting the use of conversion therapy by medical professionals. The ruling emphasizes that conversion therapy lacks scientific validity and can cause harm. Legal analysts anticipate increased scrutiny of related counseling services. Advocacy groups described the decision as a victory for LGBTQ+ health rights.

articles.title: Study finds dangerous lead levels in children’s clothing
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers tested children's shirts from multiple retailers and found each sample exceeded U.S. safety limits for lead. The report highlights risk because young children may chew on clothing. Bright colors such as red and yellow had particularly high lead levels, which the study links to chemicals used to fix dyes. Simulations suggested that even brief mouthing could expose children to unsafe lead amounts associated with harm to brain development and behavior.

articles.title: Common pesticide may more than double Parkinson’s disease risk
articles.summary_4_sentences: A UCLA Health study linked long-term exposure to the pesticide chlorpyrifos with increased Parkinson's disease risk. People living in areas with sustained exposure had more than 2.5 times the likelihood of developing the disorder. Lab experiments reported that exposed animals developed movement problems and lost dopamine-producing neurons. The study also reported toxic protein buildup similar to that seen in Parkinson's patients.
